title: The One With the Lesbian Wedding

expressions.dialogue: "It really **hit me** last night. I'm gonna be on Days of our Lives."
expressions.speaker: Joey
expressions.meaning: 어떤 사실이 갑자기 강하게 실감되다, 뒤늦게 깨닫다
expressions.nuance: 단순한 'I realized'보다 감정적 충격이 동반된 깨달음. 시간 차를 두고 뒤늦게 온다는 뉘앙스가 강함. 지금도 일상에서 매우 자주 씀.
expressions.example: I was in the shower when it **hit me** — I had completely forgotten his birthday. → 샤워하다가 갑자기 실감이 왔어 — 그의 생일을 완전히 잊고 있었다는 걸.

occ.speaker: Ross
occ.text: Yes, it was horrible. She cried. I cried. She threw things, they hit me. Anyway, I did the right thing.

occ.speaker: Rachel
occ.text: (knocking it down instead of catching it) That almost hit me in the face.

occ.speaker: Joey
occ.text: Get out! I couldn't stop if a meteor hit me.

expressions.dialogue: "which is really cool, **seeing as** I've never catered before"
expressions.speaker: Monica
expressions.meaning: ~인 걸 감안하면, ~이니까 (because / given that의 구어체)
expressions.nuance: since보다 가볍고 구어적. 약간 방어적이거나 상황을 설명할 때 자주 씀. 지금도 일상 대화에서 흔하게 등장.
expressions.example: That's actually impressive, **seeing as** you've only been coding for six months. → 코딩 시작한 지 6개월밖에 안 됐다는 걸 감안하면 사실 대단한 거야.

occ.speaker: Ross
occ.text: No, it's good, it is good, it's just that- mm- doesn't she seem a little angry?

occ.speaker: Joey
occ.text: ...Ninety-five, ninety-six, ninety-seven. See, I told you! Less than a hundred steps from our place to here.

occ.speaker: Phoebe
occ.text: (seeing Rachel's clothes) What a neat idea. All your clothes match. I'm gonna do this.

expressions.dialogue: "she taught me all about how to work the cameras, and **smell-the-fart acting**."
expressions.speaker: Joey
expressions.meaning: 대사를 떠올리는 척하며 진지한 표정으로 멈추는 과장된 연기 스타일 (Joey가 창안한 표현)
expressions.nuance: Joey가 만든 허구적 용어지만 실제로 드라마틱 포즈를 비꼬는 말로 지금도 쓰임. 연기 비평이나 유머 맥락에서 가끔 등장.
expressions.example: Every time he forgot his line, he just did the **smell-the-fart** pause and stared into the distance. → 대사를 잊을 때마다 그는 그냥 방귀 냄새 맡는 표정으로 허공을 응시했어.

occ.speaker: Joey
occ.text: Yeah, and oh she's really nice too. She taught me all about how to work the cameras, and smell-the-fart acting.

occ.speaker: Joey
occ.text: Oh yeah, and she's really nice too! She taught me about y'know, how to work with the cameras and smell-the-fart acting.

expressions.dialogue: "I can't believe you're so **uptight** about your mom comin'."
expressions.speaker: Joey
expressions.meaning: 지나치게 긴장하거나 경직된, 별거 아닌 것에 과민 반응하는
expressions.nuance: 상대가 과도하게 스트레스를 받는다고 가볍게 비판할 때 씀. 'tense'보다 더 구어적이고 비판적 뉘앙스. 지금도 흔히 쓰임.
expressions.example: Don't be so **uptight** — it's just a casual dinner, not a performance review. → 그렇게 긴장하지 마, 편한 저녁 식사지 업무 평가가 아니잖아.

occ.speaker: Rachel
occ.text: (Resenting the truth) I am not a baby! You know what? I swear to god, just because you get so uptight every time we...

occ.speaker: Phoebe
occ.text: Well, I didn't get embarrassed running next to Miss (panting). But no, okay. No, no, I can see why running with me would be embarrassing to you. Yeah, okay. You're uptight.

occ.speaker: Roy
occ.text: Well, look - it's not my fault if you're too uptight to appreciate the male form in all it's glory.

expressions.dialogue: "What would it say about me if I couldn't **revel in** your joy? I'm revelling baby, believe me!"
expressions.speaker: Ross
expressions.meaning: 무언가를 매우 즐기다, 기쁨에 흠뻑 빠지다
expressions.nuance: 다소 격식체·문학적 표현. Ross는 여기서 반어적으로 씀. 지금도 쓰이지만 일상보다는 글이나 연설에 가까운 뉘앙스.
expressions.example: She absolutely **revels in** the attention she gets when she walks into a room. → 그녀는 방에 들어설 때 받는 시선을 정말 즐겨.

expressions.dialogue: "I realize that you **have issues with** Carol and Susan, and I feel for you, I do."
expressions.speaker: Monica
expressions.meaning: ~에 대해 감정적 문제나 갈등을 갖고 있다
expressions.nuance: 1990년대 심리치료 언어가 일상화된 표현. 'have a problem with'보다 더 심리적·감정적 뉘앙스. 지금도 쓰이지만 살짝 90년대 느낌이 있음.
expressions.example: She clearly **has issues with** her manager but won't talk about it directly. → 그녀는 분명히 매니저에 대한 감정적 앙금이 있는데 직접 말하진 않아.

occ.speaker: Phoebe
occ.text: Wow. So, okay, maybe that means that, you're not over Ross yet and you have issues with your father.

expressions.dialogue: "you just come in here, and **drop this bomb** on me, before you even tell Daddy."
expressions.speaker: Rachel
expressions.meaning: 충격적이거나 예상치 못한 소식을 갑자기 알리다
expressions.nuance: 'drop a bombshell'도 같은 뜻으로 쓰임. 감정적으로 당황스러운 상황에 씀. 지금도 매우 흔하게 쓰임.
expressions.example: He **dropped a bomb** on the team by announcing he was leaving the company. → 그가 회사를 떠난다고 발표하며 팀에 폭탄을 투하했어.

expressions.dialogue: "You want me to **talk you out of** it?"
expressions.speaker: Rachel
expressions.meaning: 누군가를 설득해 어떤 것을 하지 않도록 막다
expressions.nuance: 반대인 'talk someone into'와 세트로 알아두면 좋음. 구어적이고 자주 쓰임. 지금도 매우 흔함.
expressions.example: I almost quit the program, but my advisor **talked me out of** it. → 거의 프로그램을 그만둘 뻔했는데 지도교수가 말려서 못 그랬어.

occ.speaker: Phoebe
occ.text: Yeah, but if I do tell him, then he's gonna hate myself. I mean look at him and his Mom, I can't. (pause) But, you guys can, please you gotta talk him out of it.

occ.speaker: Chandler
occ.text: (laughs) Okay, we have to talk. I'm just getting out of a very serious relationship...

occ.speaker: Chandler
occ.text: (talking out of the side of his mouth) I'm not mad.

expressions.dialogue: "I think we're **calling off** the wedding."
expressions.speaker: Carol
expressions.meaning: 이미 계획된 것을 취소하다
expressions.nuance: 'cancel'보다 구어적이며 이미 준비·진행 중인 것(결혼, 파업, 약속 등)에 씀. 지금도 매우 흔하게 쓰임.
expressions.example: They **called off** the meeting because half the team was out sick. → 팀 절반이 아파서 회의를 취소했어.

occ.speaker: Jill
occ.text: (awkwardly) Would you like to call somebody? (offering phone)

occ.speaker: Chandler
occ.text: So, it's a typical day at work. I'm inputting my numbers, and big Al calls me into his office and tells me he wants to make me processing supervisor.

occ.speaker: Monica
occ.text: I'm at work, ordinary day, you know, chop chop chop, sauti, sauti, sauti. All of a sudden, Leon, the manager, calls me into his office. It turns out they fired the head lunch chef, and guess who got the job.

expressions.dialogue: "Ross, I thought you were **over** this."
expressions.speaker: Monica
expressions.meaning: 감정적으로 극복하다, 더 이상 신경 쓰지 않다
expressions.nuance: 이별, 과거 상처, 이미 지난 일 등에 씀. 치료 언어에서 왔지만 지금은 매우 일상적. 'Are you over it yet?'처럼 단독으로도 씀.
expressions.example: I thought he was **over** the rejection, but he brought it up again. → 거절당한 걸 극복한 줄 알았는데 또 꺼내더라고.

occ.speaker: Joey
occ.text: "Warden, in five minutes my pain will be over. But you'll have to live with the knowledge that you sent an honest man to die."

occ.speaker: Ross
occ.text: Okay, Pheebs: Right hand blue. (Phoebe has to bend over.)Good. (Joey stares at her butt appreciatively)

occ.speaker: Ursula
occ.text: Yeah. Okay great. I'm gonna be over here. (She wanders away.)

expressions.dialogue: "[to Joey] **Worth a shot**, huh?"
expressions.speaker: Mr. Adelman
expressions.meaning: 시도해볼 만하다, 밑져야 본전이다
expressions.nuance: 결과가 어떻든 한번 해볼 만하다는 가벼운 뉘앙스. 실패를 각오하고 시도할 때 씀. 지금도 매우 자주 쓰임.
expressions.example: I'm not sure they'll say yes, but it's **worth a shot** to ask for a raise. → 승낙할지 모르겠지만 연봉 인상을 요청해볼 만은 해.

occ.speaker: Stanley
occ.text: Eh, worth a shot. (Gets into his car.) Look Joey, let me know where you're staying, okay? (The car peels away.)

occ.speaker: Monica
occ.text: It is going to be okay! (Mrs. Green glances over her shoulder and glares at Monica while she heads for the bathroom.) It was worth a shot.

expressions.dialogue: "I mean, she obviously has some kind of **unfinished business**."
expressions.speaker: Phoebe
expressions.meaning: 아직 해결되지 않은 일, 마무리되지 않은 것
expressions.nuance: 원래 비즈니스 용어였지만 감정·관계·삶의 과제에 비유적으로도 씀. 영적·심리적 맥락에서도 자주 등장. 지금도 흔히 씀.
expressions.example: He went back to his hometown feeling like he still had **unfinished business** there. → 그는 아직 해결하지 못한 일이 있다는 느낌에 고향으로 돌아갔어.

expressions.dialogue: "you did **cut into** my busy day of sitting"
expressions.speaker: Mr. Adelman
expressions.meaning: 누군가의 시간이나 활동을 방해하다, 끊어먹다
expressions.nuance: 여기선 Mr. Adelman이 'busy day of sitting'이라는 농담과 함께 써서 유머를 만듦. 'cut into my time/schedule' 형태로 지금도 씀.
expressions.example: Sorry to **cut into** your lunch break, but can we talk for a few minutes? → 점심시간에 끼어들어서 미안한데 잠깐 얘기할 수 있어?

occ.speaker: Phoebe
occ.text: Well, I'm returning a call from a certain mom at the B-E-A-C-H. I just spelled the wrong word. (goes into Monica's room)

occ.speaker: Joey
occ.text: But hey, listen just so you know, you might have you're work cut out for you. 'Cause when I talked to her, I kinda got the feeling that she's into some other guy. So...

occ.speaker: Ross
occ.text: It came in the mail today, it's uh, 72 long-stemmed red roses, one for each day that I've known and loved Emily, cut up into mulch!

expressions.dialogue: "if George and Adelaide can't accept that, then **the hell with them**."
expressions.speaker: Ross
expressions.meaning: ~는 신경 꺼, ~따위는 상관없어 (강한 무시·거절의 표현)
expressions.nuance: 'to hell with'도 같은 뜻. 격식체에는 맞지 않지만 드라마·일상에서 감정이 고조될 때 자주 씀. 경미한 욕설 포함.
expressions.example: **The hell with** the criticism — just submit the work and see what happens. → 비판 따위 신경 꺼 — 그냥 제출하고 뭔 일이 생기나 봐.

occ.speaker: Joey
occ.text: The hell with hockey, let's all do that!

occ.speaker: Rachel
occ.text: Yeah, who's gonna eat all our food, and tie up our phone lines, and - is that my bra? What the hell you doin' with my bra?

occ.speaker: Mr. Greene
occ.text: ...what the hell does she want with half a boat...

expressions.dialogue: "three other girls **made eyes at** me over the buffet."
expressions.speaker: Mrs. Green
expressions.meaning: 눈빛으로 추파를 던지다, 시선으로 낭만적 관심을 표현하다
expressions.nuance: 약간 구식 표현이라 나이 든 캐릭터에 자연스러움. 지금은 'flirt with' 또는 'give someone the eye'가 더 흔하게 쓰임.
expressions.example: The guy at the next table kept **making eyes at** her the whole evening. → 옆 테이블 남자가 저녁 내내 그녀에게 눈빛으로 추파를 던졌어.

occ.speaker: The Housekeeper
occ.text: More turkey Mr. Chandler? (And he makes eyes at him.)

occ.speaker: Female Clerk
occ.text: Y'know, there are two people who could put your picture up there. (She makes eyes at him.)

expressions.dialogue: "I **couldn't help but** overhear what you just said."
expressions.speaker: wedding guest (woman)
expressions.meaning: ~하지 않을 수 없었다, 어쩔 수 없이 ~했다
expressions.nuance: 자신의 행동을 정중하게 사과하거나 설명할 때 씀. 'overhear'와 특히 자주 짝을 이룸. 지금도 일상에서 자주 씀.
expressions.example: I **couldn't help but** laugh when he tripped over his own feet mid-sentence. → 말하다가 자기 발에 걸려 넘어지는 거 보고 웃지 않을 수 없었어.

occ.speaker: Joey
occ.text: That's right I stepped up! She's my friend and she needed help! And if I had too, I'd pee on anyone of you! Only, uhh, I couldn't. I got the stage fright. I wanted to help, but there was too much pressure. So-so I uh, I turned to Chandler.

occ.speaker: Amy
occ.text: um... listen, I couldn't help but overhear... 'cause I was trying to... Listen, let me do this alright? I really wanna help you guys out, and plus Rachel's been so wonderful to me... (looks at hem pleadingly)

expressions.dialogue: "it's time for you to forget about Rose, **move on with your life**."
expressions.speaker: woman at wedding
expressions.meaning: 과거에 집착하지 않고 앞으로 나아가다
expressions.nuance: 'get over it'보다 미래지향적 뉘앙스. 이별·상실·실패 후 회복을 권유할 때 씀. 지금도 매우 흔하게 쓰임.
expressions.example: Losing the contract was tough, but it's time to **move on** and focus on the next opportunity. → 계약을 잃은 건 힘들었지만 이제 앞으로 나아가서 다음 기회에 집중할 때야.

occ.speaker: Phoebe
occ.text: Well, he made a move on me.

occ.speaker: Joey
occ.text: He's right, man. Please. Move on. Go to China. Eat Chinese food.

occ.speaker: Ross
occ.text: I know, I know I was, but there was always this little voice inside that kept saying it's never gonna happen, move on. You know whose voice that was?

expressions.dialogue: "Is it a living room? Is it a restaurant? Who can tell? But I guess that's the fun." / Rachel: "**Pretty much**."
expressions.speaker: Rachel
expressions.meaning: 거의, 대체로, 그런 셈이지
expressions.nuance: 완전한 동의는 아니지만 대략 맞다는 뉘앙스. 단독 대답으로도, 문장 안에서도 씀. 지금도 매우 흔하게 쓰임.
expressions.example: Is the report done? — **Pretty much**, I just need to add the conclusion. → 보고서 다 됐어? — 거의, 결론만 추가하면 돼.

occ.speaker: Chandler
occ.text: Yeah, I think for us, kissing is pretty much like an opening act, y'know? I mean it's like the stand-up comedian you have to sit through before Pink Floyd comes out.

occ.speaker: Pizza Guy
occ.text: No, pretty much just a towel.

occ.speaker: Bob
occ.text: Yep. Pretty much.

expressions.dialogue: "How come I'm **stuck** dicing, when he gets to ball the melon?"
expressions.speaker: Chandler
expressions.meaning: 하기 싫은 일을 어쩔 수 없이 해야 하는 상황에 처하다
expressions.nuance: 불만이나 억울함을 표현할 때 씀. 'stuck with'는 원치 않는 것을 떠맡았을 때, 'stuck doing'은 하기 싫은 행동에 씀. 지금도 매우 흔함.
expressions.example: I ended up **stuck** doing all the data entry while everyone else went to lunch. → 다들 점심 먹으러 간 사이에 나만 데이터 입력을 다 하는 신세가 됐어.

expressions.dialogue: "I realize that you have issues with Carol and Susan, and I **feel for** you, I do."
expressions.speaker: Monica
expressions.meaning: ~의 처지가 안타깝다, 진심으로 공감하다
expressions.nuance: 'I feel sorry for'는 동정, 'I feel for'는 감정 이입에 가까운 뉘앙스. 더 따뜻하고 진정성 있는 표현. 지금도 씀.
expressions.example: I really **feel for** anyone who had to work from home with young kids during the pandemic. → 팬데믹 때 어린 아이들과 집에서 일해야 했던 사람들이 진심으로 안타까워.

occ.speaker: Ross
occ.text: That that's ridiculous! I don't feel guilty for her failures!

occ.speaker: Ross
occ.text: Um.... no. See, I might've had feelings for her at one time-not any more. I just-I...

occ.speaker: Monica
occ.text: If it makes anybody feel better, then we can just forget the thing, and we'll just do the gift.

expressions.dialogue: "**As in**, 'I now pronounce you wife and wife' married?"
expressions.speaker: Ross
expressions.meaning: 즉, 구체적으로 말하면 (믿기 어려운 사실을 예시로 되물을 때)
expressions.nuance: 믿기 어렵거나 확인하고 싶은 상황을 구체적 예시로 되묻는 구어체. 'You mean...?'와 유사. 지금도 매우 흔하게 씀.
expressions.example: You quit? **As in**, packed your things and walked out the door? → 그만뒀다고? 즉, 짐 싸서 그냥 나온 거야?

expressions.dialogue: "**I don't get it**. They already live together, why do they need to get married?"
expressions.speaker: Ross
expressions.meaning: 이해가 안 된다, 납득이 가지 않는다
expressions.nuance: 'I don't understand'의 구어체. 단순한 이해 부족뿐 아니라 납득이 안 된다는 뉘앙스도 포함. 지금도 매우 흔하게 씀.
expressions.example: **I don't get it** — why would anyone pay extra for slower shipping? → 이해가 안 가 — 왜 더 느린 배송에 추가 요금을 내는 거야?

occ.speaker: Rachel
occ.text: Okay, see, see, you guys, what if we don't get magic beans? I mean, what if all we've got are.. beans?

occ.speaker: Joey
occ.text: I don't get it. What happened? What about everything you said under the bridge?

occ.speaker: Chandler
occ.text: I don't get it, I mean, you just got him. How can he be an adult already?

expressions.dialogue: "They're supposed to **give me away** and everything."
expressions.speaker: Carol
expressions.meaning: 결혼식에서 신부를 신랑에게 인도하다 (전통적으로 아버지의 역할)
expressions.nuance: 전통 결혼식 관행에서 나온 표현. 지금은 성별 무관하게 쓰이기도 함. 별개로 'give away a secret'(비밀 누설)이라는 뜻도 있으니 맥락 확인 중요.
expressions.example: Her older brother **gave her away** at the ceremony since her father had passed. → 아버지가 돌아가셔서 오빠가 결혼식에서 그녀를 신랑에게 인도했어.

occ.speaker: Phoebe
occ.text: Give her a break, it's hard being on your own for the first time.

occ.speaker: Carol
occ.text: Give me a 'for instance'.

occ.speaker: Monica
occ.text: Look, I don't even know how I feel about him yet. Just give me a chance to figure that out.

expressions.dialogue: "nothing makes God happier than when two people, any two people, **come together** in love."
expressions.speaker: Minister
expressions.meaning: 하나로 모이다, 연합하다, 힘을 합치다
expressions.nuance: 결혼식 등 공식 맥락에서는 격식체로, 일반 맥락에서는 협력이나 화합을 강조할 때 씀. 비틀즈 노래로도 유명. 지금도 씀.
expressions.example: The entire neighborhood **came together** to support the family after the fire. → 화재 이후 동네 전체가 그 가족을 돕기 위해 힘을 합쳤어.

occ.speaker: Monica
occ.text: So what, you're not the only one. I mean, half the time we don't know where we're going. You've just gotta figure at some point it's all gonna come together, and it's just gonna be... un-floopy.

occ.speaker: Joey
occ.text: Come on, we were great together. And not just at the fun stuff, but like, talking too.

occ.speaker: Jason
occ.text: Yeah. I mean y'know, we haven't been going out that long. Come on, we haven't even slept together yet. Huh.

expressions.dialogue: "**In my day**, divorce was not an option."
expressions.speaker: Phoebe (Mrs. Adelman's voice)
expressions.meaning: 내가 젊었을 때에는, 예전 그 시절에는
expressions.nuance: 나이 든 사람이 과거를 현재와 비교할 때 씀. 때로는 보수적 관점, 때로는 향수를 표현. 유머로도 자주 쓰임. 지금도 씀.
expressions.example: **In my day**, you had to go to the library just to look up a single fact. → 우리 때는 사실 하나 찾으려고 도서관에 가야 했어.

occ.speaker: Fake Monica
occ.text: Monica, I started my day by peeing in front of twenty-five other women, and you're worried about who's gonna take you to the Big Apple Circus?

occ.speaker: Joey
occ.text: Alright, you're on. I can take two minutes out of my day to kick your ass.

occ.speaker: Rachel
occ.text: What's that song? It has been in my head all day long.
